$comment: ADVERSARIAL: byte-equality is the defense. An attacker who claims a particular canonical-bytes sha256 must actually have produced bytes that hash to that value — the spec's whole point is that the digest commits to the entire field set. These vectors document two tamper attempts and pin the CORRECT digest a conformant impl reaches from the (genuine) inputs, plus the WRONG digest the attacker is trying to pass off. Conformance: actual == expected_correct, AND actual != claimed.

vectors.name: envelope_body_tamper_after_signing
vectors.description: Attacker observed a signed request, then attempted to replay it with a different body — but the signature commits to canonical-bytes-of-original-body. A conformant impl recomputes canonical bytes from the request actually received, reaches the digest below, and compares against the digest the signature covers. The attacker's 'claimed_digest_sha256_hex' is the sha256 of an unrelated string ('totally-different-bytes') — included to make 'actual != claimed' an actual byte-comparison check, not a hand-wave.

vectors.name: receipt_capability_tag_strip
vectors.description: Attacker constructed a receipt input with the capability tag 'cloister/credential-isolation/v1' STRIPPED — trying to cross-replay a receipt as if it were from a different (cheaper-to-forge?) capability. The tag is load-bearing: a conformant impl ALWAYS prepends the tag, so the resulting digest differs from the attacker's tag-less digest by 100% of the bits. claimed_digest is the sha256 of the tag-less concatenation; expected_correct is the proper tagged sha256.
